coredata.dc:title: Full-scale experimental study of the characteristics of electric vehicle fires process and response measures
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Chenxi
Hu, Wenhao
Meng, Di
Mi, Wenzhong
Wang, Xuehui
Wang, Jian
coredata.dc:description: Fire accidents are a source of concern for electric vehicles (EV). The spread of fire caused by thermal runaway (TR) in a scenario with a high concentration of EVs can result in a serious disaster. In this study, a full-scale EV fire experiment was conducted using thermal abuse to investigate response strategies to EV fires caused by TR. The pattern of TR fires spreading in EV was also investigated. The suppression capabilities of fire blankets, water spray, and compressed air foam on EV fires in the early stages were analyzed. During the test, the temperature distributions inside and outside the vehicle were measured. The maximum temperature recorded in the passenger compartment near the roof was 919.7 °C. The length of the jet flow was estimated from the images, with the longest being approximately 2.56 m. The fire suppression test demonstrated that the fire blankets effectively extinguished flames, and the temperature inside the car gradually decreased. A water mist device can reduce temperature and put out flames. The compressed air foam effectively extinguished the fire, with an average cooling rate of −9.8 °C/s. These results provide information on EV fire response strategies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electric vehicle
Lithium-ion battery
Fire suppression
Fire blanket
Water mist
Compressed air foam

Electric heating pad placement of the battery pack.

Timeline of the experimental procedure.
Ignite evolution process.
Paths of flame and smoke propagation (early stage).

The longest jet flame.
Variation curve of Jet flame length.
EV covered with fire blanket.
Temperature plot during fire blanket covering (2800 s–3800 s).

Process of use and remove fire blanket.

Summary of extinguishing device parameters.

row.entry: Size: 6 m × 8 m; Material: High-Silica glass fiber

Rated pressure: 10 MPa; Rated flow: 20 L/min;
Jet distance: 13.5 m.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Maximum temperature of the bunches outside the car.

head.ce:title: Full-scale experimental study of the characteristics of electric vehicle fires process and response measures

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hefei Institute for Public Safety Research, Tsinghua University, Hefei 230601, ChinaStructured

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fire accidents are a source of concern for electric vehicles (EV). The spread of fire caused by thermal runaway (TR) in a scenario with a high concentration of EVs can result in a serious disaster. In this study, a full-scale EV fire experiment was conducted using thermal abuse to investigate response strategies to EV fires caused by TR. The pattern of TR fires spreading in EV was also investigated. The suppression capabilities of fire blankets, water spray, and compressed air foam on EV fires in the early stages were analyzed. During the test, the temperature distributions inside and outside the vehicle were measured. The maximum temperature recorded in the passenger compartment near the roof was 919.7 °C. The length of the jet flow was estimated from the images, with the longest being approximately 2.56 m. The fire suppression test demonstrated that the fire blankets effectively extinguished flames, and the temperature inside the car gradually decreased. A water mist device can reduce temperature and put out flames. The compressed air foam effectively extinguished the fire, with an average cooling rate of −9.8 °C/s. These results provide information on EV fire response strategies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Worldwide, the use of electric vehicles (EVs) is growing with a focus on environmental preservation. EVs will be sold in large numbers— approximately 10.2 million—by 2022. Globally, there are about 25.9 million EVs in use [1]. Most electric vehicles use lithium-ion batteries owing to their high energy density, extended cycle life, and lack of the memory effect [2]; however, thermal runaway (TR) in lithium-ion batteries may result in safety concerns [3]. TR is a phenomenon that occurs when a lithium-ion battery enters an uncontrollable self-heating state that can result in extremely high temperatures, violent battery discharge, toxic gases, and fire [4]. TR can occur as a result of extreme conditions such as mechanical, electrical, and thermal abuse [5–7]. An EV battery system comprises many individual cells. When one of these cells suffers thermal runaway, the heat generated is transferred to neighboring cells, which can lead to a chain of thermal runaway throughout the battery module [8]. The flame from the TR battery packs can spread to the entire EV, resulting in an EV fire. While electric vehicles are rapidly evolving, the development of fire protection systems and accident response strategies for EVs remain far behind. Effectively suppress EV fires and stop the spread of hazards in fire scenarios with a high density of vehicles, such as parking lots, tunnels, or storage vessels is crucial [16]. Therefore, research into the characteristics of EV fires and fire suppression measures is crucial.

Some EV fire tests have focused on fire hazards and their characteristics. Amandine et al. [11] measured heat release rate of two EVs based on
O 2
consumption with a maximum heat release rate (HRR) of 4.2 MW and 4.7 MW. Cui et al. [12–14] conducted a series of full-sized EV fire experiments. In these experiments, the battery pack discharged white smoke before the EV was fired under thermal abuse conditions. They found that compared with internal combustion engine vehicles, EVs spewing flames promoted flame propagation. Kang et al. [15] physically disassembled an electric car and tested the battery pack and body separately to determine their combustion parameters. Owing to the high energy density of lithium-ion batteries, EV fires have high flame intensity, rapid spread, and potential for explosions. Meanwhile, the lithium-ion battery electrode material can generate oxygen at high temperatures, increasing the difficulty of fire extinguishment and the risk of re-ignition [9,10]. Dry chemicals, carbon dioxide, and foam are commonly used to extinguish battery fires because they reduce the risk of electrocution [17]. The National Fire protection Association's (NFPA) Electric Vehicle Emergency Field Guide, a large, sustained volume of water can extinguish EV fires in most cases, which can only be supported by the fire service force [18]. However, fire services may not arrive immediately following an EV fire. To prevent a fire from spreading, measures must be taken to limit its spread before firefighters arrive. Fire blankets and water mist are two simple methods for extinguishing fires. Kwon et al. [19] used a car fire blanket without water in a car fire test, where the car fire blanket was applied during the peak of the fire. Consequently, the fire was successfully extinguished by using a fire blanket. Xie et al. [20] extinguished the fire of a large-capacity ternary lithium-ion battery used in EVs using a mobile fine water mist device, and the maximum cooling rate reached −26.9 °C/s.

Owing to the high cost of conducting fire experiments using full-sized EVs, few EV fire tests are available. To investigate response strategies in the early stages of EV fires, a full-scale EV fire experiment was conducted in an open space with three types of suppression measures. The characteristics of the EV fire process were analyzed, and the effectiveness of the three suppression measures was evaluated. These results are expected to provide information on emergency response plans for EV hazards.

The EV used in the test was a sedan passenger car with full functionality and a maximum mileage of 4000 km. The side windows with a width of approximately 10 cm were left open. The battery cells were composed of the NCM ternary material. The battery pack has 16 battery modules, each with 12 cells, and its total battery capacity is 38.1 kWh. The battery pack was original and located in the chassis directly under the rear seat of the vehicle (see Fig. 1 ). A steel armor plate separated the passenger compartment from the battery pack.

As shown in Fig. 2 , an electric heating pad (400 W) was used to trigger the TR of the EV battery pack. If the rate of heat generation exceeds the rate of heat dissipation to the surroundings, the temperature will rise due to the presence of an external heating source. The battery thermally discharges when a critical temperature is reached, particularly the separator's breakdown temperature [21].

Two HD cameras were placed on the sides and front of the vehicle. An infrared camera was set up on the rear, diagonal of the vehicle. K-type thermocouples were arranged around the vehicle and its interior. The experimental data were collected at 1-s intervals. The specific locations of the sensors are depicted in Fig. 3 .

A total of 37 K-type thermocouples were used to record the temperature variations. Two thermocouples (A1–A4 and B1–B5) were used to measure the temperature in the interior of the vehicle. Four thermocouples (C0–C6, D0–D6, E0–E6, and F0–F6) were used to measure the flame temperature in the exterior space of the vehicle. Bunch A was equipped with four thermocouples, bunch B received five, and the remaining bunches were equipped with seven thermocouples. The two adjacent thermocouples on each branch were separated by 20 cm. Bunches A and B, placed inside the vehicle, were uniformly distributed between the roof and floor of the passenger compartment. Both bunches were mounted on the centerline of the vehicle. Bunch A was mounted between two front seats. Bunch B was placed in front of a seat in the rear passenger compartment. Bunch C, Bunch D, Bunch E and Bunch F were placed at 0.7 m standoff distances from the car body, which corresponded to the distance between vehicles in the parking lot. The branch's lowest thermocouple was located 25 cm above ground level.

In this experiment, an electric car fire was triggered using an electric heating pad inside the battery pack. After the fire spread, the blanket, fine water spray, and high-pressure foam were placed sequentially. Finally, the flames were extinguished, and the battery pack was cooled continuously. The parameters of the three devices are listed Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Combustion behavior of EV fires process
ce:section.ce:para: When the fire spreads, it blankets the surface of the car body in flames. The blanket that had been removed in the event of a fire was successfully suppressed, and the temperature change curve inside the vehicle began to fall. When the fire blanket was removed, the vehicle quickly re-ignited. Following that, the fine water mist device was used to observe the effect of fine water mist on the flames. The water mist reduced smoke and cooled the area, but it did not extinguish the flames. The fine water mist was removed around 3900 s, and firefighters used compressed air foam to extinguish the fire. The battery pack was cooled with water after the flame had completely extinguished.
Similar to previous studies, the TR ignition phase of an EV battery pack produces white smoke, hot sparks, and jet flames [22]. The crack was located in the back, right near the heating pad.

According to estimates, half of all jet fires can cause at least one additional event that exacerbates the accident [25,26]. As shown in Fig. 9, the length of the jet flame was measured using image processing with the width of the car as a reference. Four prominent jet flames are observed before the fire blanket is covered. Unfortunately, the temperature of the jet flame could not be obtained because the thermocouple and jet fire were on opposite sides of the EV. The first jet occurred 17.1 s after ignition, lasted 5.7 s and had a maximum length of 2.067 m. The second jet was 10.6 s later and lasted 9.9 s with the longest jet flame reaching 2.564 m. The third jet occurred 9.8s later, with a duration of 19.7 s and a maximum length of 1.742 m. The fourth jet occurred 15.8 s later, lasted 17 s and had a maximum length of 1.562 m (see Fig. 8
).

The maximum length of the jet fire (2.564 m) was comparable to the results of Cui et al. [13]. If this jet flame is considered a turbulent gas jet, the flame length does not change as the fuel flow increases. Its length depends on the nature of the fuel and diameter of the nozzle [27]. Hawthorne et al. [28] established the following relationship for estimate the length of a free-turbulent flame jet:
L D
=
5.3
C τ
T F
α T
T N
[
C τ
+
(
1 −
C τ
)
M S
M N
]
where
T F
is adiabatic flame temperature,
T N
is absolute temperature of nozzle fluid
M S
,
M N
is molecular weights of surrounding and nozzle fluids, respectively
C τ
is mol fraction of nozzle fluid in the unreacted stoichiometric mixture
α T
is mols of reactants/mols products, for the stoichiometric mixture.

A simplified semi-empirical formula has been developed for engineering applications:
L D
= 6
(
R + 1
)
(
ρ e
ρ F
)
1 2
where R is air-fuel ratio,
ρ e
is fuel gas density,
ρ F
is flame gas density.

The gas species and generation rate varied with the temperature during thermal runaway. Let us assume a mixture with a constant composition of 41.2 % CO2, 30.8 % H2, 13.0 % CO, 8.2 % C2H4 and 6.8 % CH4 [29]. From the gas properties it is found that R = 3.24,
ρ e
= 1.083 kg/
m 3
. Flame gas density is approximately the same for most combustible gases, where
ρ F
= 0.205 kg/
m 3
at 1400 °C. The nozzle diameter was derived from the maximum observed value of the jet flame, and the calculation yielded D = 0.043 m. Nozzle shape was not considered [30].

A fire blanket is a protective film that prevents the expansion of combustion from spreading into surrounding combustibles. In most fire scenarios, cutting off air entry can prevent combustion propagation. As cutting off air cannot extinguish lithium-ion battery fires, fire blankets can isolate EVs from the outside world and prevent fires from spreading. The effectiveness of fire blankets has been demonstrated in automobile fire experiments conducted by various manufacturers [31]. In this experiment, a fire blanket was applied only 132 s after the EV had caught fire, which is faster than any other known tests (see Fig. 10 ).

Fig. 11 shows the temperature curve during the fire blanket coverage. The temperature outside the car dropped immediately to near-ambient levels when the fire blankets were arranged. The flames were effectively extinguished using a fire blanket. Subsequently, the temperature fluctuated slightly depending on the amount of smoke emitted. Before using the blanket, the temperature inside the car was maintained at 25–30°, the same as the outside temperature. After fire blanket coverage, the temperature inside the vehicle suddenly increased as a result of the fire blanket preventing the hot smoke vent from opening the windows. The maximum peak temperature measured by the thermocouple was about 600 °C. The temperature in the rear passenger compartment was higher than that in the cockpit. This phenomenon was observed in a previous study [32]. There was distinct temperature stratification, indicating that no turbulent combustion occurred inside the vehicle) (see.

When the fire blanket was removed, the temperature dropped as hot smoke dissipated and cool air flowed in. The EV quickly re-ignited after the flammable gas and air mixed together and the temperature rose quickly.

When the fire blanket is removed, a large amount of white smoke is emitted. After 57 s, re-ignition occurred, and the white smoke changed to dark gray smoke. It is clear that the burning and flames were much more intense than those before the fire blanket was covered.

Water mist technology has been recognized as a viable alternative to halons in a variety of fire-fighting applications. Fire tests on the battery packs were used to extrapolate its ability to suppress EV fires [34]. Previous studies relied heavily on static water-mist spraying devices [35]. This experiment used a movable water mist spray equipment that firefighters had to operate.

After the flashover in the passenger compartment, the flow rate of the fine water mist was inadequate for the fire, but the cooling effect continued. Compared to the temperature curve that reached over 900 °C after the fine water mist device removed, the temperature in the passenger compartment was limited to about 800 °C during the fine water mist application (see Fig. 15).

In the experiment, the spout of the water mist was set at the rear of the vehicle. Therefore, the rear area of the vehicle was more affected by the influence of the water mist than the front area. The water mist had a significant effect on lowering the temperature peak (see Table 2 ).

In previous full-scale experiments, the flame would quickly spread to the engine compartment. In this experiment, the battery pack had a denser rear battery arrangement than the front, and TR occurred from the rear. Therefore, the rear of the vehicle had a stronger fire in the early stages of fire development. The fire initially spread to the rear due to natural winds. The rear of the vehicle, closest to the fire source point, reached its peak temperature first, followed by the front of the vehicle. The fine water mist was mostly sprayed from the top downwards, and in this experiment, the mobile fine water mist could only be operated from one side. The driving force of the water spray affects the flame spreading direction and speed. The scale of the fire increased due to the accelerated spread of the fire to unburned areas (see Fig. 16 ).

There are two aspects to the effect of fine water mist on fires. A portion of the water mist is removed by the smoke without vaporization, so the number of water mist particles reaching the flame surface in the high-temperature area is greatly reduced. In addition, the mixing process of the fuel gas and oxygen is accelerated, which reduces the cooling effect of the fine water mist. Furthermore, water mist impinges on the flame and increases the mixing area between the oxidizer and fuel [38].

Fires in passenger compartments can be classified into two stages, based on the onset of the flashover. A flashover was thought to occur in the passenger compartment after the fire spread into it (see Fig. 17 ), and the fire gained strength. After about 50 s, the windshield broke. More adequate contact between the air and combustible gases caused a rapid increase in temperature [23]. When flashover occurred, the maximum temperature in the passenger compartment reached 576 °C. Subsequently, the fire died out and the temperature dropped to 270 °C. The temperature rose to 800 °C when the rear windshield broke. The maximum temperature recorded was 929 °C.

There are two main sources of heat that contribute to the peak of an electric vehicle fire. The heat released from the battery's thermal runaway and the combustible gases that burn in the car's trunk and chassis make up one portion of the heat. The other involves the passenger compartment's interior materials being burned, creating flammable gases that escape through the windows and cause a fire plume above the car's roof (see Fig. 18 ). The battery pack's fumes may enter the cockpit if the armor plate in the chassis burns.

The height of the fire plume was measured using an installed staff gauge as reference (see Fig. 19 ). The maximum height observed was 2.8 m above the ground. According to Heskestad's empirical formula [24], the flame heat release rate can be estimated from the flame height. The passenger compartment was assumed to be a square fire source with 1.8 m side length located at 0.7 m height. The heat release rate of the flame was estimated to be 1.14 MW.

The firefighters stepped in at 3910 s. The flame rapidly decreased when the compressed air foam was used (see Fig. 20 ). However, the battery continued to emit white gas smoke, necessitating additional water for an extended period of time.

Compressed air foam was then used. The temperatures inside and outside the damaged vehicle rapidly decreased after the compressed air foam was used. Fig. 21 shows the temperature curves of the front seat. According to the temperature curve of thermocouple A3 from 3930 s to 4021 s, the average cooling rate is around −9.8 °C/s, maximum cooling rate is −28.4 °C/s.

The experiment was conducted outdoors and lacked the experimental conditions needed to determine the peak heat release rate (pHRR) and total heat released (THR). Some of the thermocouples failed early in the experiment after thermal runaway occurred, resulting in loss of critical data. Some conclusions, however, can be drawn.
(1)
The fire was comparatively small before spreading to the passenger compartment. In the early stages, jet fire is a concern, and the longest jet fire observed in this experiment was 2.56 m.
(2)
The fire intensified after reaching the passenger compartment. The fire peak was dominated by passenger-compartment combustion. Limiting the fire before it reaches the passenger compartment can significantly reduce its environmental impact. As the battery pack and passenger compartment are zones where combustible materials are concentrated and prone to fire, good fire separation can retard the spread of fire and save time for fire response.
(3)
Fire blankets can effectively extinguish flames and control the spread of fire in the early stages, but they have little effect on stopping the battery thermal runaway. The gas produced by the battery is collected in a car. Therefore, gaps in the combined section and heat burning through the blanket must be avoided. After removing the fire blanket, the fire was quickly rekindled. In general, fire blankets can effectively stop the spread of a disaster, but cannot terminate it.
(4)
Fine water mist can help to reduce the temperature and heat release rate of the flame. However, its low flow rate cannot extinguish fires in the passenger compartment or stop the thermal runaway of the battery. The kinetic energy of the fine water mist device affects the spread of fire in the car body. It may make sense to run water mist through existing openings in a car body.
(5)
Compressed air foam took 90 s to extinguish the fire; the average cooling rate is around −9.8 °C/s. However, the reactions in the battery are still active and require cooling for a long time.
Chenxi Zhao: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing original draft preparation.
Wenhao Hu: Investigation.
Di Meng: Investigation.
Wenzhong Mi: Project administration.
Xuehui Wang*: Project administration, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.
Jian Wang *: Supervision, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Funding: This study was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (grant number 2022YFC3006300). Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2021B1515130008). Open Foundation of the Key Laboratory of Fire Protection Technology for Industry and Public Buildings (2022KLIB07).

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: R. T. Cotts, "Best Practices for Emergency Response to Incidents Involving Electric Vehicles Battery Hazards: A Report on Full-Scale Testing Results," Fire Protection Research Foundation.

Yang, "The studies on structural and thermal properties of delithiated LixNi1/3Co1/3Mn1/3O2 (0 < x <= 1) as a cathode material in lithium ion batteries," Solid State Ionics, Article vol. 177, no. 17-18, pp. 1509-1516, Jul 2006, doi: 10.1016/j.ssi.2006.03.055.
